title: Kitten or Cat Dragging Hind Legs: Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: If your cat is dragging its back legs, it is a medical emergency. Learn about potential causes like saddle thrombus and why you must see a vet now.
content_html:
Understanding Hind Limb Paralysis

When a cat or kitten suddenly loses the ability to use their back legs, it is a condition known as posterior paralysis or paresis. This often happens suddenly and can be caused by various issues, including Feline Aortic Thromboembolism (also known as a saddle thrombus), spinal cord trauma, or severe neurological infections. In many cases, the cat may appear distressed, vocalize loudly, or have limbs that feel cold to the touch.

Urgency Level: High Emergency

This is considered a critical veterinary emergency. If your cat is dragging their hind limbs, you should not wait for an appointment or observe them at home. Immediate intervention is required to manage pain and address life-threatening causes such as blood clots or spinal compression. Time is of the essence to improve the chances of recovery and prevent permanent nerve damage.

Why a Video or Photo Helps Triage

If you can safely do so without delaying your trip to the emergency room, capturing a quick video of your cat attempting to move can be invaluable. When cats arrive at the clinic, their adrenaline often masks symptoms or alters their gait. A video provides the veterinary team with a clear, objective record of the event in a home environment, allowing them to more accurately differentiate between neurological weakness and physical injury.

faqs.question: Is dragging hind legs always a sign of pain?
faqs.answer: In most sudden cases, such as a blood clot, it is extremely painful for the cat. Even if the cat is not vocalizing, paralysis is a high-stress medical event that requires professional pain management.

faqs.question: What is a saddle thrombus?
faqs.answer: A saddle thrombus is a blood clot that lodges at the base of the aorta, cutting off blood flow to the hind legs. It is a common cause of sudden paralysis in cats and is a life-threatening emergency.

faqs.question: Can I give my cat human aspirin for the pain?
faqs.answer: No. Never administer human pain medications to a cat. Many are highly toxic to felines and can lead to organ failure or death, complicating their emergency treatment.
